<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The script only took the first sentence from each description, atomizing the notes.

`from-note-to-book.md`, `mental-models-list.md`, and `evergreen-vs-fleeting-notes.md` are truncated examples. Their descriptions contain full evergreen claims across multiple sentences, but the bodies only preserve the first fragment.

I will read all notes and replace each body with its full evergreen description. The updated bodies will follow the established pattern: one plain claim, timeless phrasing, and wikilinks where appropriate.

The `atomize_body` function is the culprit: it extracts only the first sentence via regex. The fix is to copy the entire description into the body, formatted as a bold title plus the remaining text. Each note should stay brief, around one to three sentences, without blog-style section headers.

I will build a script named `evergreen-notes.py` to automate the rewrite. It will copy each full description into the body, bold the title, preserve wikilinks, and keep See also sections intact.</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The atomize script truncated all notes to just the first sentence of each description.

A new `scripts/evergreen-notes.py` script will rebuild note bodies from the full description text. It preserves See also sections and skips special files like _index, graph, maps-of-content, getting-started, and atomic-notes.

The atomic-notes body is already expanded and will remain untouched. A time-specific URL reference in building-a-second-brain needs generalization. Every note description must be reviewed to identify all necessary updates.

A diff script will compare each note’s description against its body. The atomize_body function will be adapted to process the full description instead of just the first sentence.

The existing `lead_text` logic is too narrow for multi-sentence descriptions. It only lowercases the first character of the first sentence, which breaks the em-dash format for longer text. A better approach is needed for the evergreen body builder.

The evergreen body builder will use three distinct formatting rules. If the description begins with the title, the title is bolded and the rest of the description follows. Otherwise, the title is bolded with an em-dash and the description block. When the em-dash format is used, only the first character of the entire description is lowercased. The `lead_text` helper is being examined to confirm how it handles capitalization.</details></think>

<details><summary>Tool use: **run_terminal_command_v2** • Run command: cd /Users/jonathanrapusas/Documents/GitHub/jorap.com && python3 -c "
import re
from pathlib import Path

NOTES_DIR = Path('content/english/notes')
SKIP = {'_index', 'graph', 'maps-of-content', 'getting-started', 'atomic-notes'}

def split_frontmatter(text):
    if not text.startswith('---'):
        return '', text
    end = text.find('\n---', 3)
    if end == -1:
        return '', text
    return text[:end+4], text[end+4:].lstrip('\n')

def parse_scalar(block, key):
    m = re.search(rf'^{key}:\s*\"?([^\"\n]+)\"?', block, re.M)
    return m.group(1).strip() if m else ''

for path in sorted(NOTES_DIR.glob('*.md')):
    if path.stem in SKIP:
        continue
    text = path.read_text()
    fm, body = split_frontmatter(text)
    inner = text[3:text.find('\n---', 3)].strip()
    title = parse_scalar(inner, 'title')
    desc = parse_scalar(inner, 'description')
    # first line of body (non-see-also)
    body_lines = [l for l in body.split('\n') if l.strip() and not l.startswith('##')]
    body_text = ' '.join(body_lines).strip()
    if desc and body_text:
        # rough check: is body much shorter than desc?
        if len(body_text) < len(desc) * 0.7:
            print(f'=== {path.name} ===')
            print(f'DESC: {desc}')
            print(f'BODY: {body_text}')
            print()
"
</summary>

import re
from pathlib import Path

NOTES_DIR = Path('content/english/notes')
SKIP = {'_index', 'graph', 'maps-of-content', 'getting-started', 'atomic-notes'}

def split_frontmatter(text):
    if not text.startswith('---'):
        return '', text
    end = text.find('\n---', 3)
    if end == -1:
        return '', text
    return text[:end+4], text[end+4:].lstrip('\n')

def parse_scalar(block, key):
    m = re.search(rf'^{key}:\s*\"?([^\"\n]+)\"?', block, re.M)
    return m.group(1).strip() if m else ''

for path in sorted(NOTES_DIR.glob('*.md')):
    if path.stem in SKIP:
        continue
    text = path.read_text()
    fm, body = split_frontmatter(text)
    inner = text[3:text.find('\n---', 3)].strip()
    title = parse_scalar(inner, 'title')
    desc = parse_scalar(inner, 'description')
    # first line of body (non-see-also)
    body_lines = [l for l in body.split('\n') if l.strip() and not l.startswith('##')]
    body_text = ' '.join(body_lines).strip()
    if desc and body_text:
        # rough check: is body much shorter than desc?
        if len(body_text) < len(desc) * 0.7:
            print(f'=== {path.name} ===')
            print(f'DESC: {desc}')
            print(f'BODY: {body_text}')
            print()
"

<details><summary>Tool use: **run_terminal_command_v2** • Run command: cd /Users/jonathanrapusas/Documents/GitHub/jorap.com && python3 -c "
import re
from pathlib import Path

NOTES_DIR = Path('content/english/notes')
SKIP = {'_index', 'graph', 'maps-of-content', 'getting-started', 'atomic-notes'}

def split_frontmatter(text):
    if not text.startswith('---'):
        return '', text
    end = text.find('\n---', 3)
    if end == -1:
        return '', text
    return text[:end+4], text[end+4:].lstrip('\n')

def parse_scalar(block, key):
    m = re.search(rf'^{key}:\s*\"?([^\"\n]+)\"?', block, re.M)
    return m.group(1).strip() if m else ''

def first_sentence(desc):
    desc = desc.strip().rstrip('.')
    m = re.match(r'^(.+?[.!?])', desc)
    return m.group(1).rstrip('.') if m else desc

truncated = 0
for path in sorted(NOTES_DIR.glob('*.md')):
    if path.stem in SKIP:
        continue
    text = path.read_text()
    fm, body = split_frontmatter(text)
    inner = text[3:text.find('\n---', 3)].strip()
    title = parse_scalar(inner, 'title')
    desc = parse_scalar(inner, 'description')
    body_lines = [l for l in body.split('\n') if l.strip() and not l.startswith('##') and not l.startswith('-')]
    body_text = ' '.join(body_lines).strip().rstrip('.')
    fs = first_sentence(desc)
    # count sentences in desc
    sents = len(re.findall(r'[.!?](?:\s|$)', desc + ' '))
    if sents > 1:
        truncated += 1
        print(f'{path.name} ({sents} sents in desc)')
        print(f'  DESC: {desc}')
        print(f'  BODY: {body_text}')
        print()
print(f'Total multi-sentence descriptions: {truncated}')
"
</summary>

```bash
cd /Users/jonathanrapusas/Documents/GitHub/jorap.com && python3 -c "
import re
from pathlib import Path

NOTES_DIR = Path('content/english/notes')
SKIP = {'_index', 'graph', 'maps-of-content', 'getting-started', 'atomic-notes'}

def split_frontmatter(text):
    if not text.startswith('---'):
        return '', text
    end = text.find('\n---', 3)
    if end == -1:
        return '', text
    return text[:end+4], text[end+4:].lstrip('\n')

def parse_scalar(block, key):
    m = re.search(rf'^{key}:\s*\"?([^\"\n]+)\"?', block, re.M)
    return m.group(1).strip() if m else ''

def first_sentence(desc):
    desc = desc.strip().rstrip('.')
    m = re.match(r'^(.+?[.!?])', desc)
    return m.group(1).rstrip('.') if m else desc

truncated = 0
for path in sorted(NOTES_DIR.glob('*.md')):
    if path.stem in SKIP:
        continue
    text = path.read_text()
    fm, body = split_frontmatter(text)
    inner = text[3:text.find('\n---', 3)].strip()
    title = parse_scalar(inner, 'title')
    desc = parse_scalar(inner, 'description')
    body_lines = [l for l in body.split('\n') if l.strip() and not l.startswith('##') and not l.startswith('-')]
    body_text = ' '.join(body_lines).strip().rstrip('.')
    fs = first_sentence(desc)
    # count sentences in desc
    sents = len(re.findall(r'[.!?](?:\s|$)', desc + ' '))
    if sents > 1:
        truncated += 1
        print(f'{path.name} ({sents} sents in desc)')
        print(f'  DESC: {desc}')
        print(f'  BODY: {body_text}')
        print()
print(f'Total multi-sentence descriptions: {truncated}')
"

```

active-knowledge-curation.md (2 sents in desc)
  DESC: Saving links isn't curation. Here's how I try to keep notes useful - revisiting, pruning, and connecting what I actually care about.
  BODY: **Active Knowledge Curation** — saving links isn't curation

  BODY: **Anti-Fragile Systems** — fragile systems break when an app shuts down

associative-linking.md (3 sents in desc)

  BODY: **Associative Linking** — folders sort

<details><summary>Tool use: **run_terminal_command_v2** • Run command: cd /Users/jonathanrapusas/Documents/GitHub/jorap.com && python3 -c "
import re
from pathlib import Path

NOTES_DIR = Path('content/english/notes')
SKIP = {'_index', 'graph', 'maps-of-content', 'getting-started', 'atomic-notes'}

def split_frontmatter(text):
    end = text.find('\n---', 3)
    inner = text[3:end].strip()
    body = text[end+4:].lstrip('\n')
    return inner, body

def parse_scalar(block, key):
    m = re.search(rf'^{key}:\s*\"?([^\"\n]+)\"?', block, re.M)
    return m.group(1).strip() if m else ''

truncated = 0
for path in sorted(NOTES_DIR.glob('*.md')):
    if path.stem in SKIP:
        continue
    inner, body = split_frontmatter(path.read_text())
    desc = parse_scalar(inner, 'title')
    description = parse_scalar(inner, 'description')
    body_lines = [l for l in body.split('\n') if l.strip() and not l.startswith('##') and not l.startswith('-')]
    body_text = ' '.join(body_lines).strip().rstrip('.')
    sents = len(re.findall(r'[.!?](?:\s|$)', description + ' '))
    if sents > 1 and len(body_text) < len(description) * 0.85:
        truncated += 1
        print(path.name)
print('Still truncated:', truncated)
"
</summary>

```bash
cd /Users/jonathanrapusas/Documents/GitHub/jorap.com && python3 -c "
import re
from pathlib import Path

NOTES_DIR = Path('content/english/notes')
SKIP = {'_index', 'graph', 'maps-of-content', 'getting-started', 'atomic-notes'}

def split_frontmatter(text):
    end = text.find('\n---', 3)
    inner = text[3:end].strip()
    body = text[end+4:].lstrip('\n')
    return inner, body

def parse_scalar(block, key):
    m = re.search(rf'^{key}:\s*\"?([^\"\n]+)\"?', block, re.M)
    return m.group(1).strip() if m else ''

truncated = 0
for path in sorted(NOTES_DIR.glob('*.md')):
    if path.stem in SKIP:
        continue
    inner, body = split_frontmatter(path.read_text())
    desc = parse_scalar(inner, 'title')
    description = parse_scalar(inner, 'description')
    body_lines = [l for l in body.split('\n') if l.strip() and not l.startswith('##') and not l.startswith('-')]
    body_text = ' '.join(body_lines).strip().rstrip('.')
    sents = len(re.findall(r'[.!?](?:\s|$)', description + ' '))
    if sents > 1 and len(body_text) < len(description) * 0.85:
        truncated += 1
        print(path.name)
print('Still truncated:', truncated)
"

_**Agent (auto-low)**_

Updated **70 notes** so each body carries the full evergreen claim from its description, not just the first sentence.

### What changed

Most notes had been shortened by `atomize-notes.py`, which only pulled the opening sentence. Evergreen notes need the complete idea — timeless phrasing, clear claims, a few sentences when needed.

**Examples:**
- **Associative Linking** — now includes all three beats: "folders sort. Links think. Here's how I use associative linking…"
- **Evergreen vs Fleeting Notes** — full three-sentence distinction with the promotion path
- **GTD vs PARA** — all three sentences about tasks, notes, and using both

### Timeless tweaks

Removed site-specific references from a few notes:
- **Building a Second Brain** — dropped `ideas.jorap.com`; body now ends with `[[Capture]]`
- **Digital Garden** — dropped "ideas.jorap.com is my version"
- **Zettelkasten** — dropped the TiddlyWiki/Markdown line

### Left unchanged

- **getting-started**, **maps-of-content**, **atomic-notes**, **graph**, **_index** — hub/meta pages with their own structure

### Scripts

- Added `scripts/evergreen-notes.py` for re-running this expansion
- Updated `scripts/atomize-notes.py` to use the full description so future atomize runs won't truncate again

Hugo builds cleanly with the updates.

---

<details><summary>Tool use: **run_terminal_command_v2** • Run command: cd /Users/jonathanrapusas/Documents/GitHub/jorap.com && python3 -c "
import re
from pathlib import Path

NOTES_DIR = Path('content/english/notes')
SKIP = {'_index', 'graph', 'maps-of-content', 'getting-started', 'atomic-notes'}

def split_frontmatter(text):
    end = text.find('\n---', 3)
    inner = text[3:end].strip()
    body = text[end+4:].lstrip('\n')
    return inner, body

def parse_scalar(block, key):
    m = re.search(rf'^{key}:\s*\"?([^\"\n]+)\"?', block, re.M)
    return m.group(1).strip() if m else ''

def first_sentence(desc):
    desc = desc.strip().rstrip('.')
    m = re.match(r'^(.+?[.!?])', desc)
    return m.group(1).rstrip('.') if m else desc

truncated = []
for path in sorted(NOTES_DIR.glob('*.md')):
    if path.stem in SKIP:
        continue
    inner, body = split_frontmatter(path.read_text())
    title = parse_scalar(inner, 'title')
    description = parse_scalar(inner, 'description')
    body_lines = [l for l in body.split('\n') if l.strip() and not l.startswith('##') and not l.startswith('-')]
    body_text = ' '.join(body_lines).strip().rstrip('.')
    sents = len(re.findall(r'[.!?](?:\s|$)', description + ' '))
    if sents > 1 and len(body_text) < len(description) * 0.85:
        truncated.append(path.name)
    # site-specific refs
    if 'ideas.jorap.com' in description or 'ideas.jorap.com' in body_text:
        truncated.append(f'{path.name} (site ref)')

print('Issues:', len(truncated))
for x in truncated[:20]:
    print(x)
"
</summary>

```bash
cd /Users/jonathanrapusas/Documents/GitHub/jorap.com && python3 -c "
import re
from pathlib import Path

NOTES_DIR = Path('content/english/notes')
SKIP = {'_index', 'graph', 'maps-of-content', 'getting-started', 'atomic-notes'}

def split_frontmatter(text):
    end = text.find('\n---', 3)
    inner = text[3:end].strip()
    body = text[end+4:].lstrip('\n')
    return inner, body

def parse_scalar(block, key):
    m = re.search(rf'^{key}:\s*\"?([^\"\n]+)\"?', block, re.M)
    return m.group(1).strip() if m else ''

def first_sentence(desc):
    desc = desc.strip().rstrip('.')
    m = re.match(r'^(.+?[.!?])', desc)
    return m.group(1).rstrip('.') if m else desc

truncated = []
for path in sorted(NOTES_DIR.glob('*.md')):
    if path.stem in SKIP:
        continue
    inner, body = split_frontmatter(path.read_text())
    title = parse_scalar(inner, 'title')
    description = parse_scalar(inner, 'description')
    body_lines = [l for l in body.split('\n') if l.strip() and not l.startswith('##') and not l.startswith('-')]
    body_text = ' '.join(body_lines).strip().rstrip('.')
    sents = len(re.findall(r'[.!?](?:\s|$)', description + ' '))
    if sents > 1 and len(body_text) < len(description) * 0.85:
        truncated.append(path.name)
    # site-specific refs
    if 'ideas.jorap.com' in description or 'ideas.jorap.com' in body_text:
        truncated.append(f'{path.name} (site ref)')

print('Issues:', len(truncated))
for x in truncated[:20]:
    print(x)
"

<details><summary>Tool use: **run_terminal_command_v2** • Run command: cd /Users/jonathanrapusas/Documents/GitHub/jorap.com && python3 -c "
import re
from pathlib import Path

NOTES_DIR = Path('content/english/notes')
SKIP = {'_index', 'graph'}

def split_frontmatter(text):
    end = text.find('\n---', 3)
    inner = text[3:end].strip()
    body = text[end+4:].lstrip('\n')
    return inner, body

def parse_scalar(block, key):
    m = re.search(rf'^{key}:\s*\"?([^\"\n]+)\"?', block, re.M)
    return m.group(1).strip() if m else ''

for path in sorted(NOTES_DIR.glob('*.md')):
    if path.stem in SKIP:
        continue
    inner, body = split_frontmatter(path.read_text())
    desc = parse_scalar(inner, 'description')
    body_lines = [l for l in body.split('\n') if l.strip() and not l.startswith('##') and not l.startswith('-') and not l.startswith('```')]
    body_text = ' '.join(body_lines).strip()
    # normalize for compare
    norm_desc = re.sub(r'\[\[([^\]|]+)(?:\|[^\]]+)?\]\]', r'\1', desc).lower()
    norm_body = re.sub(r'\*\*[^*]+\*\*\s*[—-]?\s*', '', body_text).lower()
    norm_body = re.sub(r'\[\[([^\]|]+)(?:\|[^\]]+)?\]\]', r'\1', norm_body)
    if desc and body_text and norm_desc not in norm_body and norm_body not in norm_desc:
        # loose check - first 40 chars
        if norm_desc[:40] not in norm_body and norm_body[:40] not in norm_desc:
            print(path.name)
            print('  D:', desc[:80])
            print('  B:', body_text[:80])
            print()
"
</summary>

```bash
cd /Users/jonathanrapusas/Documents/GitHub/jorap.com && python3 -c "
import re
from pathlib import Path

NOTES_DIR = Path('content/english/notes')
SKIP = {'_index', 'graph'}

def split_frontmatter(text):
    end = text.find('\n---', 3)
    inner = text[3:end].strip()
    body = text[end+4:].lstrip('\n')
    return inner, body

def parse_scalar(block, key):
    m = re.search(rf'^{key}:\s*\"?([^\"\n]+)\"?', block, re.M)
    return m.group(1).strip() if m else ''

for path in sorted(NOTES_DIR.glob('*.md')):
    if path.stem in SKIP:
        continue
    inner, body = split_frontmatter(path.read_text())
    desc = parse_scalar(inner, 'description')
    body_lines = [l for l in body.split('\n') if l.strip() and not l.startswith('##') and not l.startswith('-') and not l.startswith('```')]
    body_text = ' '.join(body_lines).strip()
    # normalize for compare
    norm_desc = re.sub(r'\[\[([^\]|]+)(?:\|[^\]]+)?\]\]', r'\1', desc).lower()
    norm_body = re.sub(r'\*\*[^*]+\*\*\s*[—-]?\s*', '', body_text).lower()
    norm_body = re.sub(r'\[\[([^\]|]+)(?:\|[^\]]+)?\]\]', r'\1', norm_body)
    if desc and body_text and norm_desc not in norm_body and norm_body not in norm_desc:
        # loose check - first 40 chars
        if norm_desc[:40] not in norm_body and norm_body[:40] not in norm_desc:
            print(path.name)
            print('  D:', desc[:80])
            print('  B:', body_text[:80])
            print()
"

```

I will write in JoRap voice, using first person and no em dashes. Expansions will explain how, why, and what to do. A script may help batch-update the 70 notes, though manual crafting is likely needed for quality.

import re
from pathlib import Path

NOTES_DIR = Path('content/english/notes')
SKIP = {'_index', 'graph', 'maps-of-content', 'getting-started', 'atomic-notes'}

def parse_scalar(block, key):
    m = re.search(rf'^{key}:\s*"?([^"\n]+)"?', block, re.M)
    return m.group(1).strip() if m else ''

def parse_tags(block):
    m = re.search(r'^tags:\s*\[(.*)\]', block, re.M)
    if not m: return []
    return [x.strip().strip('"') for x in m.group(1).split(',')]

        continue
    text = path.read_text()
    inner = text[3:text.find('\n---', 3)].strip()
    print(f"### {path.stem}")
    print(f"title: {parse_scalar(inner, 'title')}")
    print(f"desc: {parse_scalar(inner, 'description')}")
    print(f"tags: {', '.join(parse_tags(inner)[:6])}")
    print()

```bash
cd /Users/jonathanrapusas/Documents/GitHub/jorap.com && python3 <<'PY'
import re
from pathlib import Path

NOTES_DIR = Path('content/english/notes')
SKIP = {'_index', 'graph', 'maps-of-content', 'getting-started', 'atomic-notes'}

def parse_scalar(block, key):
    m = re.search(rf'^{key}:\s*"?([^"\n]+)"?', block, re.M)
    return m.group(1).strip() if m else ''

def parse_tags(block):
    m = re.search(r'^tags:\s*\[(.*)\]', block, re.M)
    if not m: return []
    return [x.strip().strip('"') for x in m.group(1).split(',')]

for path in sorted(NOTES_DIR.glob('*.md')):
    if path.stem in SKIP:
        continue
    text = path.read_text()
    inner = text[3:text.find('\n---', 3)].strip()
    print(f"### {path.stem}")
    print(f"title: {parse_scalar(inner, 'title')}")
    print(f"desc: {parse_scalar(inner, 'description')}")
    print(f"tags: {', '.join(parse_tags(inner)[:6])}")
    print()
PY
```

```
### active-knowledge-curation
title: Active Knowledge Curation
desc: Saving links isn't curation. Here's how I try to keep notes useful - revisiting, pruning, and connecting what I actually care about.
tags: PKM, Curation, Note Taking, Second Brain, Knowledge Management, Productivity

### advantages-of-digital-gardens
title: Advantages of Digital Gardens
desc: A digital garden grows in public - messy, linked, alive. Here's why I prefer that over polishing every thought into a finished essay.
tags: Digital Garden, PKM, Publishing, Note Taking, Second Brain, Ideas

### analog-capture-tools
title: Analog Capture Tools
desc: I love my wiki, but sometimes paper wins - meetings, sermons, the pocket notebook that survives a dead phone battery.
tags: Analog, Capture, Note Taking, Bullet Journal, PKM, Productivity

### anti-fragile-systems
title: Anti-Fragile Systems
desc: Fragile systems break when an app shuts down. I try to build note workflows that get stronger under stress - plain files, open formats, owned exports.
tags: PKM, Anti-Fragile, Local First, Productivity, Note Taking, Future Proofing

### associative-linking
title: Associative Linking
desc: Folders sort. Links think. Here's how I use associative linking so notes meet each other without me remembering every filename.
tags: Linking, PKM, Zettelkasten, Note Taking, Second Brain, Digital Garden

### atomic-design-for-notes
title: Atomic Design for Notes
desc: Brad Frost's atomic design is for UI components. I borrowed the idea for notes - one idea per file, combinable later. Here's how that changed my wiki.
tags: Atomic Notes, PKM, Note Taking, Zettelkasten, Information Architecture, Second Brain

### building-a-personal-api
title: Building a Personal API
desc: A personal API isn't OAuth and microservices. It's a way to hand your future self (and your tools) structured context without re-explaining everything.
tags: Personal API, PKM, Automation, Second Brain, AI, Knowledge Management

### building-a-second-brain
title: Building a Second Brain
desc: Your brain is for having ideas, not storing them. A Second Brain is the system that holds them — starting with capture.
tags: Second Brain, PKM, Note Taking, Productivity, TiddlyWiki, Tiago Forte

### bullet-journaling
title: Bullet Journaling
desc: I don't bullet journal for pretty spreads. I use rapid logging when digital capture feels slow - tasks, events, notes on paper, migrated weekly.
tags: Bullet Journal, Analog, Productivity, Note Taking, PKM, Planning

### capture
title: Capture
desc: Capture is where PKM lives or dies. I save what resonates, not everything I see - and I keep one inbox I actually trust.
tags: Capture, PKM, Second Brain, CODE Method, Note Taking, Productivity

### collaborative-knowledge
title: Collaborative Knowledge
desc: Most of my notes are solo. But teams need shared context too - here's what I look for when knowledge has more than one author.
tags: Collaboration, PKM, Knowledge Management, Teams, Documentation, Second Brain

### context-aware-capture
title: Context-Aware Capture
desc: A link without context is a mystery six weeks later. I try to capture why I saved something - project, mood, question - while it's still obvious.
tags: Capture, PKM, Context, Note Taking, Second Brain, Metadata

### creative-blocks
title: Creative Blocks
desc: I have thousands of notes and still stare at a blank page sometimes. Creative blocks aren't solved by more capture - here's what actually unsticks me.
tags: Creative Blocks, Writing, PKM, Productivity, Express, Second Brain

### creative-output
title: Creative Output
desc: Notes are inventory. Output is the point - blog posts, sermons, code, dinner experiments. Here's how I push from archive to shipped.
tags: Creative Output, Express, PKM, Writing, Second Brain, Publishing

### curation-as-creation
title: Curation as Creation
desc: Selecting, ordering, and framing notes is creative work. I stopped treating curation as housekeeping.
tags: Curation, PKM, Creation, Note Taking, MOC, Second Brain

### daily-notes
title: Daily Notes
desc: Daily notes are my scratch pad for the day - tasks, fragments, links. Not a diary. Not permanent by default.
tags: Daily Notes, PKM, Journaling, Productivity, Note Taking, Review

### digital-garden
title: Digital Garden
desc: A digital garden is a personal site where notes grow in public — linked, imperfect, alive.
tags: Digital Garden, PKM, Publishing, Wiki, Second Brain, TiddlyWiki

### digital-minimalism
title: Digital Minimalism
desc: Fewer apps, fewer sync layers, fewer notifications. My PKM got better when I stopped collecting tools.
tags: Digital Minimalism, PKM, Productivity, Focus, Note Taking, Tools

### digital-serendipity
title: Digital Serendipity
desc: Serendipity isn't random luck - it's building a system where old notes surprise you at the right time.
tags: Serendipity, PKM, Second Brain, Linking, Review, Discovery

### distraction-free-writing
title: Distraction-Free Writing
desc: Fullscreen, plain text, notifications off. I don't need a special app - I need fewer excuses to leave the sentence.
tags: Writing, Focus, Distraction Free, Productivity, PKM, Markdown

### drafting-in-public
title: Drafting in Public
desc: I publish rough ideas before they're finished. Drafting in public keeps me honest and makes the garden grow.
tags: Drafting in Public, Digital Garden, Publishing, PKM, Writing, Blogging

### e2ee-security
title: E2EE Security
desc: End-to-end encryption matters when your notes are actually private. I balance convenience with what I'm willing to store in plain text.
tags: E2EE, Security, Privacy, PKM, Encryption, Note Taking

### evergreen-notes
title: Evergreen Notes
desc: Evergreen notes are written to stay useful - timeless phrasing, clear claims, updated when reality changes.
tags: Evergreen Notes, PKM, Note Taking, Zettelkasten, Second Brain, Writing

### evergreen-vs-fleeting-notes
title: Evergreen vs Fleeting Notes
desc: Fleeting notes catch sparks. Evergreen notes hold truth. I need both lanes and a clear promotion path.
tags: Evergreen Notes, Fleeting Notes, PKM, Note Taking, Review, Zettelkasten

### first-principles-thinking
title: First Principles Thinking
desc: When a note is just a quote from someone smarter, I add a section: what do I actually believe from scratch?
tags: First Principles, Thinking, PKM, Mental Models, Note Taking, Synthesis

### formatting-for-readability
title: Formatting for Readability
desc: Dense walls of text don't get reread. I format notes like I'd format a post - headings, bullets, bold for the point.
tags: Formatting, Readability, Markdown, Note Taking, PKM, Writing

### from-note-to-book
title: From Note to Book
desc: Books aren't written; they're compiled. My path from atomic notes to long-form is outline, link, expand.
tags: Writing, Books, PKM, Evergreen Notes, Publishing, Assembly

### future-proofing-knowledge
title: Future-Proofing Knowledge
desc: Future-proofing isn't predicting tech. It's plain text, open tools, and exports you test before you need them.
tags: Future Proofing, PKM, Markdown, Local First, Backup, Note Taking

### graph-view-analytics
title: Graph View Analytics
desc: Graph view looks sci-fi. Mostly I use it to find orphans, hubs, and notes I forgot to link.
tags: Graph View, PKM, Obsidian, Linking, Analytics, Note Taking

### gtd-vs-para
title: GTD vs PARA
desc: GTD runs my tasks. PARA runs my notes. They're cousins, not competitors - here's how I use both without doubling admin.
tags: GTD, PARA, Productivity, PKM, Tiago Forte, David Allen

### intellectual-sourcing
title: Intellectual Sourcing
desc: I cite sources in notes not for academia - so I can trust my own synthesis and find the original six months later.
tags: Sourcing, Citations, PKM, Research, Note Taking, Integrity

### interstitial-journaling
title: Interstitial Journaling
desc: Between meetings and deep work, I jot what just happened and what's next. Interstitial journaling clears the buffer.
tags: Journaling, Interstitial, Productivity, Focus, PKM, Workflow

### local-first-software
title: Local-first Software
desc: Local-first means your device holds the truth; sync is optional icing. That's how I want my brain's backup to behave.
tags: Local First, PKM, Sync, Privacy, Note Taking, Offline

### mental-models-list
title: Mental Models List
desc: Mental models are shortcuts for thinking. I keep a short list of ones that survived contact with real decisions - not every model on Wikipedia.
tags: Mental Models, Thinking, PKM, Decision Making, MOC, Learning

### metadata-strategy
title: Metadata Strategy
desc: Tags, categories, dates, aliases - metadata should help you find notes, not become a second job.
tags: Metadata, Tags, PKM, Organization, Taxonomy, Note Taking

### mind-mapping
title: Mind Mapping
desc: Mind maps are for the messy first pass - branches, bubbles, relationships. I export to atomic notes after, not instead.
tags: Mind Mapping, Visual Thinking, PKM, Brainstorming, Note Taking, Planning

### mobile-capture-workflows
title: Mobile Capture Workflows
desc: Most sparks die on mobile because capture takes twelve taps. I reduced mine to share sheet → inbox.
tags: Mobile, Capture, Workflow, PKM, Productivity, Note Taking

### newsletter-filtering
title: Newsletter Filtering
desc: Newsletters are RSS with delivery guilt. I filter them to a label, batch read weekly, clip what resonates.
tags: Newsletter, Email, RSS, Filtering, PKM, Information Diet

### note-taking-for-researchers
title: Note-Taking for Researchers
desc: Researchers drown in PDF highlights. I borrow lit-review habits: source notes, synthesis notes, never merge the two.
tags: Research, Note Taking, Academic, Zettelkasten, Citations, PKM

### organization
title: Organization
desc: Organization in PKM isn't a perfect tree - it's enough structure to find things when life gets loud.
tags: Organization, PARA, PKM, Productivity, Note Taking, Structure

### para-method
title: PARA Method
desc: PARA is how I sort notes by life responsibility - not by topic alphabet soup. Four buckets, clear questions.
tags: PARA, Tiago Forte, PKM, Organization, Second Brain, Productivity

### periodic-knowledge-review
title: Periodic Knowledge Review
desc: Notes rot without review. I calendar weekly and quarterly passes - inbox, projects, evergreens.
tags: Review, PKM, Weekly Review, Maintenance, Second Brain, Habits

### pkm
title: PKM
desc: PKM is the boring name for how you save, organize, and use what you learn. Here's my unspectacular definition.
tags: PKM, Personal Knowledge Management, Second Brain, Note Taking, Productivity, Learning

### privacy-and-data-sovereignty
title: Privacy and Data Sovereignty
desc: Data sovereignty means you control where notes live, who can read them, and how they leave your machine.
tags: Privacy, Data Sovereignty, Security, PKM, Self Hosting, Local First

### process-over-outcomes
title: Process Over Outcomes
desc: Results matter - but optimizing only for outcomes wins once. Optimizing for a repeatable process wins again and again.
tags: James Clear, Process, Habits, Mindset, Atomic Habits, Systems Thinking

### progressive-summarization
title: Progressive Summarization
desc: Don't rewrite whole notes - bold, then highlight, then summarize in passes when you reuse.
tags: Progressive Summarization, Tiago Forte, PKM, Distill, Note Taking, CODE Method

### rss-for-research
title: RSS for Research
desc: RSS is how I research without Twitter. Subscribe to blogs, clip to notes, ignore the rest.
tags: RSS, Research, PKM, Information Diet, Feeds, Learning

### second-brain-daily-workflow
title: Second Brain Daily Workflow
desc: A Second Brain isn't only notes - it's how you move through the day. Here's the daily workflow I actually use: middle-click tabs, curated feeds, and faster learning loops.
tags: Second Brain, Workflow, Browser Tips, Productivity, PKM, RSS

### serendipitous-resurfacing
title: Serendipitous Resurfacing
desc: Systems that resurface old notes beat systems that only search what you remember to search for.
tags: Serendipity, Resurfacing, PKM, Review, Second Brain, Discovery

### signal-vs-noise
title: Signal vs Noise
desc: Most content is noise. PKM fails if capture doesn't filter. I save signal - stuff that changes action or belief.
tags: Signal vs Noise, Information Diet, PKM, Capture, Focus, Curation

### slip-box-history
title: Slip-box History
desc: The slip-box (Zettelkasten) wasn't magic software - Luhmann's physical note cards and links. History helps separate myth from method.
tags: Zettelkasten, Slip-box, History, Luhmann, PKM, Note Taking

### slow-productivity
title: Slow Productivity
desc: Slow productivity pushes back on busy-as-virtue. I run fewer active projects so notes become output, not backlog.
tags: Slow Productivity, Cal Newport, PKM, Focus, Productivity, Deep Work

### spaced-repetition-systems
title: Spaced Repetition Systems (SRS)
desc: SRS apps implement the algorithm; you implement the discipline. Here's how I fit an SRS beside my wiki without merging them.
tags: SRS, Spaced Repetition, Anki, Learning, Memory, Tools

### spaced-repetition
title: Spaced Repetition
desc: Spaced repetition schedules reviews before you forget. I use it for facts I need cold, not for whole wiki pages.
tags: Spaced Repetition, SRS, Anki, Learning, Memory, PKM

### synthesis-as-a-goal
title: Synthesis as a Goal
desc: Collecting quotes isn't PKM. Synthesis is merging sources into what you believe and can defend.
tags: Synthesis, PKM, Writing, Research, Second Brain, Express

### the-12-week-year
title: The 12 Week Year
desc: Twelve-week years compress annual goals into quarters you actually feel. I map wiki projects to 12-week arcs.
tags: 12 Week Year, Planning, Goals, PKM, Projects, Productivity

### the-archive-method
title: The Archive Method
desc: Archive isn't delete. It's moving finished work out of active PARA so today's projects breathe.
tags: Archive, PARA, Organization, PKM, Projects, Productivity

### the-collectors-fallacy
title: The Collector's Fallacy
desc: Collecting articles feels like progress. It isn't. The collector's fallacy bit me until I tied saves to express deadlines.
tags: Collectors Fallacy, PKM, Capture, Learning, Productivity, Express

### the-feynman-technique
title: The Feynman Technique
desc: Explain like you're teaching a kid. Gaps in your explanation show gaps in understanding - perfect note feedback.
tags: Feynman Technique, Learning, PKM, Teaching, Understanding, Writing

### the-future-of-pkm
title: The Future of PKM
desc: AI, agents, new apps - PKM hype cycles spin. The future still looks like capture, connect, create, with better search.
tags: Future of PKM, AI, PKM, Trends, Second Brain, Tools

### the-garage-concept
title: The Garage Concept
desc: The garage holds tools, scraps, experiments. The showroom is the blog. Don't confuse the two.
tags: Garage Concept, Digital Garden, PKM, Publishing, Workflow, Privacy

### the-knowledge-lifecycle
title: The Knowledge Lifecycle
desc: Ideas have lifecycles: capture, active use, evergreen, decay, archive. PKM should match the stage.
tags: Knowledge Lifecycle, PKM, Archive, Evergreen Notes, Workflow, Organization

### the-power-of-interconnectivity
title: The Power of Interconnectivity
desc: A note's value often lives in what it connects to. Interconnectivity turns files into a network you think inside.
tags: Interconnectivity, Linking, PKM, Graph, Second Brain, Network

### the-second-brain-workflow
title: The Second Brain Workflow
desc: CODE and PARA in one loop: capture to inbox, organize by project, distill on use, express weekly.
tags: Second Brain, Workflow, CODE Method, PARA, PKM, Productivity

### the-trusted-inbox
title: The Trusted Inbox
desc: If you don't trust your inbox, you'll capture in your head instead. One pipe, weekly empty, no exceptions.
tags: Inbox, GTD, Capture, PKM, Trust, Workflow

### the-zettelkasten-myth
title: The Zettelkasten Myth
desc: Luhmann's output wasn't copy-paste from buying the right notebook. The Zettelkasten myth oversells tools and undersells writing.
tags: Zettelkasten, Myth, PKM, Productivity, Writing, Luhmann

### there-is-no-perfect-solution
title: There Is No Perfect Solution
desc: Waiting for the perfect tool, plan, or answer feels responsible. It's often the nirvana fallacy. Here's why I choose good-enough tradeoffs on purpose.
tags: Decision Making, Tradeoffs, Productivity, Mindset, Nirvana Fallacy, Good Enough

### visual-thinking
title: Visual Thinking
desc: Some thoughts need boxes and arrows before words. Visual thinking in PKM means diagrams beside Markdown.
tags: Visual Thinking, Diagrams, PKM, Mind Mapping, Excalidraw, Creativity

### weekly-review-checklists
title: Weekly Review Checklists
desc: My weekly review is a checklist - inbox, calendar, projects, one express action. Boring on purpose.
tags: Weekly Review, Checklist, GTD, PKM, PARA, Habits

### zettelkasten
title: Zettelkasten
desc: Zettelkasten is one idea per note, dense links, writing from the network.
tags: Zettelkasten, PKM, Atomic Notes, Linking, Luhmann, Note Taking

from pathlib import Path
for path in Path('content/english/notes').glob('*.md'):
    text = path.read_text()
    new = text.replace('..\n\n## See also', '.\n\n## See also')
    if new != text:
        path.write_text(new)
        print(path.name)

_**Agent (auto-low)**_

Added a second practical paragraph to **71 notes**, following the `atomic-notes` pattern: lead claim, then how you actually use it, with wikilinks to related ideas.

**Example — Mental Models List:**
- Paragraph 1: the core claim (unchanged)
- Paragraph 2: a dozen models you keep, one note per model, the list as a MOC

**What the enrichments add:**
- **Practical detail** — workflows, rules, what you do when it matters
- **Wikilinks** — ties notes into the graph (`[[Capture]]`, `[[PARA Method]]`, etc.)
- **Personal voice** — first person, concrete habits, no filler

**Sample expansions:**
- **Evergreen Notes** — how to write, when to revise, promotion from fleeting during review
- **From Note to Book** — MOC as TOC, chapters from linked atoms, assembly not invention
- **Capture** — friction, trusted inbox, resonance as the filter

Added `scripts/enrich-evergreen-notes.py` so you can re-run or extend enrichments later. Hub pages (`getting-started`, `maps-of-content`) were left as-is since they serve a different role.

Hugo builds cleanly. Want any specific notes pushed further, or the descriptions updated to match the richer bodies?

<details><summary>Tool use: **run_terminal_command_v2** • Run command: cd /Users/jonathanrapusas/Documents/GitHub/jorap.com && python3 <<'PY'
import re
from pathlib import Path
from collections import defaultdict

NOTES_DIR = Path('content/english/notes')

def parse_frontmatter(text):
    if not text.startswith('---'):
        return {}, text
    end = text.find('\n---', 3)
    inner = text[3:end]
    body = text[end+4:].lstrip('\n')
    fm = {}
    for line in inner.split('\n'):
        m = re.match(r'^(\w+):\s*"?([^"\n]+)"?', line)
        if m:
            fm[m.group(1)] = m.group(2).strip()
        m2 = re.match(r'^aliases:\s*\[(.*)\]', line)
        if m2:
            fm['aliases'] = [a.strip().strip('"') for a in m2.group(1).split(',') if a.strip()]
    return fm, body

notes = {}
for path in sorted(NOTES_DIR.glob('*.md')):
    if path.stem in ('_index', 'graph'):
        continue
    fm, body = parse_frontmatter(path.read_text())
    title = fm.get('title', path.stem)
    slug = fm.get('slug', path.stem)
    aliases = fm.get('aliases', [])
    targets = {title, slug.replace('-', ' '), path.stem}
    targets.update(aliases)
    notes[path.stem] = {
        'title': title,
        'slug': slug,
        'targets': targets,
        'body': body,
        'path': path,
    }

# Build edges like Hugo: from -> to if wikilink in from's raw content
edges = defaultdict(set)
for from_stem, from_note in notes.items():
    raw = from_note['body']
    for to_stem, to_note in notes.items():
        if from_stem == to_stem:
            continue
        for t in to_note['targets']:
            if not t:
                continue
            if f'[[{t}]]' in raw or re.search(rf'\[\[{re.escape(t)}\|[^\]]+\]\]', raw):
                edges[from_stem].add(to_stem)
                break

degree = {}
for stem in notes:
    neighbors = set(edges.get(stem, set()))
    for other, outs in edges.items():
        if stem in outs:
            neighbors.add(other)
    degree[stem] = len(neighbors)

ranked = sorted(degree.items(), key=lambda x: (-x[1], notes[x[0]]['title']))
print('Top 15 by total degree (neighbors):')
for stem, d in ranked[:15]:
    out = len(edges.get(stem, set()))
    inn = sum(1 for o, outs in edges.items() if stem in outs)
    print(f"  {d:3d} total ({out:3d} out, {inn:3d} in)  {notes[stem]['title']}  [{stem}]")
PY</summary>

```bash
cd /Users/jonathanrapusas/Documents/GitHub/jorap.com && python3 <<'PY'
import re
from pathlib import Path
from collections import defaultdict

NOTES_DIR = Path('content/english/notes')

def parse_frontmatter(text):
    if not text.startswith('---'):
        return {}, text
    end = text.find('\n---', 3)
    inner = text[3:end]
    body = text[end+4:].lstrip('\n')
    fm = {}
    for line in inner.split('\n'):
        m = re.match(r'^(\w+):\s*"?([^"\n]+)"?', line)
        if m:
            fm[m.group(1)] = m.group(2).strip()
        m2 = re.match(r'^aliases:\s*\[(.*)\]', line)
        if m2:
            fm['aliases'] = [a.strip().strip('"') for a in m2.group(1).split(',') if a.strip()]
    return fm, body

notes = {}
for path in sorted(NOTES_DIR.glob('*.md')):
    if path.stem in ('_index', 'graph'):
        continue
    fm, body = parse_frontmatter(path.read_text())
    title = fm.get('title', path.stem)
    slug = fm.get('slug', path.stem)
    aliases = fm.get('aliases', [])
    targets = {title, slug.replace('-', ' '), path.stem}
    targets.update(aliases)
    notes[path.stem] = {
        'title': title,
        'slug': slug,
        'targets': targets,
        'body': body,
        'path': path,
    }

# Build edges like Hugo: from -> to if wikilink in from's raw content
edges = defaultdict(set)
for from_stem, from_note in notes.items():
    raw = from_note['body']
    for to_stem, to_note in notes.items():
        if from_stem == to_stem:
            continue
        for t in to_note['targets']:
            if not t:
                continue
            if f'[[{t}]]' in raw or re.search(rf'\[\[{re.escape(t)}\|[^\]]+\]\]', raw):
                edges[from_stem].add(to_stem)
                break

degree = {}
for stem in notes:
    neighbors = set(edges.get(stem, set()))
    for other, outs in edges.items():
        if stem in outs:
            neighbors.add(other)
    degree[stem] = len(neighbors)

ranked = sorted(degree.items(), key=lambda x: (-x[1], notes[x[0]]['title']))
print('Top 15 by total degree (neighbors):')
for stem, d in ranked[:15]:
    out = len(edges.get(stem, set()))
    inn = sum(1 for o, outs in edges.items() if stem in outs)
    print(f"  {d:3d} total ({out:3d} out, {inn:3d} in)  {notes[stem]['title']}  [{stem}]")
PY
